title: Fitting a kitchen sink waste and trap
subtitle: Fit a new waste outlet, trap, and waste run under a kitchen sink
excerpt: How to fit a kitchen sink waste and trap: fitting the waste outlet and sealing washer, connecting a P-trap or bottle trap, connecting to the waste branch pipe, checking the slope, and testing for leaks.

content.text: are separate components. The waste outlet seals to the sink; the trap screws to the waste outlet tail and connects to the wall branch. Both connections must be watertight and both must have adequate slope (a minimum 1 in 40 slope on horizontal waste runs).

Apply a thin ring of plumber's putty or sanitary silicone around the underside of the waste outlet flange. Press the fitting down through the hole in the sink basin. From underneath, place the rubber washer, then the flat washer, then the back-nut onto the threaded tail. Tighten the back-nut hand-tight then a further half-turn with water-pump pliers, compressing the washer against the underside of the sink. Do not over-tighten; the washer squeezes out if the back-nut is turned more than one full turn past hand-tight.
Step 2, select and connect the trap

For tight under-sink spaces, a P-trap or S-trap with a horizontal outlet suits most installations. A bottle trap takes less vertical space but is more prone to blockage than a P-trap. Screw the trap inlet to the waste outlet tail, hand-tight then a quarter-turn. The trap must be removable by hand for clearing blockages; never use PTFE tape or sealant on trap cap threads.
Step 3, connect to the waste branch

The trap outlet connects to the 40 mm waste branch using a push-fit or solvent-weld spigot and socket. If the trap outlet is a different size, use a reducer. Check the connection is fully pushed home; a partly-engaged push-fit socket leaks as soon as the compression sleeve relaxes. If the sink is a double-bowl type, connect the second bowl with a waste connector before the trap.
Step 4, check the slope and support the pipe

Place a spirit level along the horizontal waste run. The slope should be between 1 in 40 and 1 in 80 (18 to 12 mm drop per metre). A steeper slope washes the water through too fast, leaving solids behind; a shallower slope or a flat run causes blockages. Support any horizontal run longer than 500 mm (20 in) with a pipe clip to prevent the pipe sagging over time.

Fill the sink to the overflow level and pull the plug. Watch every connection point as the water drains: the waste outlet back-nut, both trap unions, and the connection to the branch. Any drip will be visible at full flow. A slow seep at the waste outlet back-nut means the seal washer needs a further quarter-turn of tightening.

attrs.fix: Undo the back-nut, remove the fitting, reapply the putty or silicone evenly around the full flange edge, and refit. Tighten the back-nut to the correct tension.
attrs.cause: The rubber washer is not seating squarely, or the plumber's putty is not fully distributed under the flange.
attrs.symptom: Water drips from around the waste outlet after fitting

attrs.fix: Check each push-fit joint is fully seated. Re-check the slope with a spirit level along the full waste run and reposition pipe clips to achieve the correct slope.
attrs.cause: The waste run has insufficient slope, or a push-fit joint is partly collapsed, reducing the bore.
attrs.symptom: Slow drainage from the new installation
